chapters.title: Even in 2016, women still get pushed to the side
chapters.text:
Sheryl Sandberg once hosted a meeting at Facebook. She had invited fifteen executives from across Silicon Valley, and Timothy Geithner, the former U.S. Secretary of the Treasury, came with four of his staff. When Sandberg invited the mostly male guests to help themselves at the buffet, the men filled their plates and took the large tables. The women filled their plates last and sat off to the side. Sandberg walked over and invited the women to the main tables. They declined. They had been formally invited to the meeting, but the seating made them look like spectators. That single moment opened Sandberg's eyes to a truth she had been circling for years. Beyond the obvious outside barriers, women carry quiet internal ones. Many women prefer to step back and watch from a safe distance, and that habit keeps them from reaching the top of the workplace.

Self-doubt is one of the heaviest weights some women carry.

Most women tend to undervalue themselves. Studies have shown that women are quick to rate their work as worse than it really is, while men tend to rate theirs as better than it is. Men credit their wins to personal skill, while women credit theirs to outside help. When failures come, men look outward, and women look inward. The press and the public do the same thing. When Facebook prepared to go public, for example, The New York Times published a piece that said Sandberg had been lucky and had powerful mentors. The piece was not wrong, but it led with the outside factors, not with her ability.

To grow, I have to trust my own ability. I still meet moments that scare me.

Sandberg's observation lines up with what researchers call the confidence gap. Many women hold back because they are not sure they are ready, while many men charge in even when they are not ready. Over time, that small difference adds up to a real gap in pay, in promotions, and in who ends up in the corner office.

chapters.title: Pick a path that fits your life, not the crowd's voice
chapters.text:
Women have every skill they need to lead. According to the National Center for Education Statistics, girls in the United States outpace boys in school, earning 57 percent of undergraduate degrees and 60 percent of master's degrees. In spite of that, the workplace rewards risk-taking and self-promotion, traits that many girls are not pushed to develop. That gap between classroom success and career success is one of the most studied problems in business. One reason is the leadership ambition gap. Many women are ambitious and want to reach the top of their field, but the numbers tell a different story. In a 2012 McKinsey survey of 4,000 employees at successful companies, 36 percent of the men said they wanted to reach executive level, compared with only 18 percent of the women.

More women need to aim for the top jobs, because they are ready for them.

Men are far more likely to picture themselves in senior roles, and that picture forms early. Author Samantha Ettus and her husband were flipping through their daughter's yearbook, and they reached a page where children had been asked what they wanted to be when they grew up. Most of the boys said president. None of the girls did. When a man shows ambition, he is celebrated. When a woman shows the same drive, she is judged. Many women grow up believing that since men run most of the world, the top job is not for them. There is also a stubborn stereotype that a serious career woman cannot be attractive. Fear is the hidden driver behind most of the obstacles women face at work, and without that fear, they could climb much higher on the career ladder.

Without fear, women can reach the top of their field and still build a life they love, whether that means work, family, or both.

chapters.title: Internal barriers hold women back the most
chapters.text:
Sandberg returned to the same story of the Facebook meeting in her writing. The setup is identical, and the lesson is the same. In a room full of tech leaders, the women slipped to the back of the room, even after being invited forward. The moment reminded her that external obstacles are only half the story. Internal habits, the quiet choices women make about how to behave, are the other half.

Most women downplay their wins, and the habit carries a cost.

Studies confirm that most women undervalue their work, while men tend to overvalue theirs. When a man lands a big project, he is the first to take credit. When a woman lands the same project, she is more likely to thank her team or to call it luck. The press repeats the same pattern. When a woman is promoted, the story is often about how lucky she was or who her mentors were. The story rarely starts with her skill. The result is a slow drip of self-doubt that pulls women away from the bold moves that could move their careers forward.

Sandberg has written that the only way out is to bet on yourself, even when the room doubts you. The starting line of any career change is the quiet decision that you are ready.

chapters.title: A good act can look bad if a woman does it
chapters.text:
Frank Flynn and Cameron Anderson, professors at Columbia Business School and New York University, ran a study in 2003 to see how gender shapes the way we judge people at work. They used a real entrepreneur named Heidi Roizen as their subject. Flynn and Anderson split their study group into two. One half read Heidi's story, and the other half read the same story with the name changed to Howard.

Do not shrink your ambition just to fit in at work.

Afterward, the researchers asked the participants about Heidi or Howard. Both were seen as capable entrepreneurs. Yet, the participants liked Howard. Heidi, by contrast, was seen as selfish and as someone they would not want to work for. The data was identical. The only thing that changed was the gender, and the impression changed with it. When a man is successful, people cheer. When a woman is successful, people criticize, both men and women. When women excel, they are often described in unflattering terms.

Margaret Thatcher was called Attila the Hen. Indira Gandhi was called The Old Witch. When a woman becomes good at her job and stops trying to please the men around her, people say she is acting like a man, and they pull back from her. Gender stereotypes push some women to do more work for less reward. Women need to give up the need to be liked by everyone, because that need is one of the quiet reasons many women stay stuck. Real change in any field starts with accepting that you cannot please everyone.

A quick note. According to UN Women research from 2021, women serve as heads of state in 22 countries, and 119 countries have never had a female leader.

chapters.title: Earn the mentor by doing the work
chapters.text:
When Sandberg speaks at conferences, women in the audience often ask her to be their mentor. In 2011, Nitin Nohria, then the Dean of Harvard Business School, invited her to speak. During the question period, the men in the room asked sharp, business-focused questions. What lessons from her past work was she applying at Facebook? How did she keep her developers happy? Then a woman stood up and asked, how do I find a mentor? Sandberg felt a pang of embarrassment.

When you meet with a mentor, come prepared and respect their time.

The men in the room wanted answers on how to run a business. The women wanted rescue. Women are taught that with the right mentor, the climb gets easier, so they are pushed to go out and find one. The hidden cost is that the search trains them to lean on others.

Mentorship is important, but it is not something you go hunting for. It grows out of a real connection between two people. Studies show that mentors are drawn to the talent and drive of the people they help. The advice should be the other way around. Do the work, and the mentor will come. Walking up to a stranger and asking them to be your mentor rarely works. Walking up to the same person with a sharp, well-formed question almost always does. The best mentorship is a two-way street. The mentor offers experience, and the mentee offers fresh information and a sense of purpose.

Do not bring a mentor your complaints. Bring them a problem to solve. Asking for help is not weakness, it is strength. Peers can mentor each other, and the lessons from a peer can be just as valuable as lessons from a senior leader.

chapters.title: Honest feedback is a gift
chapters.text:
One common bad habit at work is dodging the truth. People stay quiet to protect themselves and the people around them. Workers in lower positions find it hard to speak up, often because they fear getting fired. For women, honesty in the office carries extra risk. They worry about being called nags or about losing the label of team player. Telling the truth is fine. Attacking someone's character is not. Use simple, direct language.

For example, a Chinese woman once tried to tell Mark Zuckerberg about problems with her manager. Zuckerberg asked her to keep it simple. She tried again, and he still asked her to simplify. Finally, the woman lost her patience and shouted that her manager was terrible. She was speaking Chinese, but Zuckerberg got the point. Telling the truth can move a company forward.

If you welcome feedback, you will grow.

Admitting a problem is the first step toward solving it. Asking others for their view of you shows you what they actually see. When you ask for feedback instead of guessing, you can work on the real gaps. Be open to criticism and ask people to name the areas you should improve. That habit is the only reliable way to get better. The truth can sting, but it pays off. Asking for feedback and being honest about your own shortcomings in public can also build trust. When people speak honestly to you, thank them in front of others and they will speak up again.

Humor helps, too. Another way to build trust is to share feelings and to give people room to be themselves at work. Cry if you need to. Someday, the things that mark women down will be a thing of the past.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
Real equality at work will only come when more women hold senior roles across every industry. Women should never have to choose between their work and their family. We can lift each other by encouraging every woman to chase leadership with confidence and grit. Employers, coworkers, and partners all have a part to play. Men, for example, can help by treating women with respect and as equals. When women come together, they get stronger. It is time for women to set big goals, push past the barriers, and reach for their full potential.

When more women sit in leadership, women get treated fairly at work. Women should release the internal habits that hold them back from chasing power. Let go of self-doubt that whispers you are not good enough. Speak up at work. Step out of the back row.

Mentors are a great resource, but it is not wise to lean on them too heavily. It is fine to ask for advice when you are stuck, and it is fine to lean on your partner when you need to. When women reach positions of power, they tend to put policies in place that help everyone. A world where every woman can reach the peak of her career without internal or external obstacles would be a beautiful one. It is time to support women with ambition, and to stop criticizing them and labeling them with ugly nicknames.

Try this

Take a few quiet minutes to picture the role you want at work. Then, if there are skills you need to sharpen to qualify, go and get the training.
